coredata.dc:title: Urate oxidase-loaded MOF electrodeposited on boron nanosheet-doxorubicin complex as multifunctional nano-enzyme platform for enzymatic and ratiometric electrochemical biosensing
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Meng
Sun, Zejun
Jin, Hui
Gui, Rijun
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a novel multifunctional nano-enzyme platform was developed and used for enzymatic and ratiometric electrochemical biosensing of uric acid (UA). Boron nanosheets (BNSs) were prepared through ultrasound-assisted liquid-phase exfoliation, followed by the loading of doxorubicin (DOX) to form BNSs-DOX complex. The complex was drop-casted on glassy carbon electrode (GCE) surface to prepare BNSs-DOX/GCE. Cobalt-based metal-organic framework (MOF) with encapsulation of urate oxidase (UOx) was in-situ copolymerized and electrodeposited on the BNSs-DOX surface to construct UOx@MOF/BNSs-DOX nanohybrid-modified GCE. The modified electrode serves as an artificial nano-enzyme sensing platform and presents multifunctional functions, including DOX-loaded BNSs carrier, UOx-enzyme immobilization, enzymatic redox and ratiometric electrochemical sensing of UA. The platform was explored as a new ratiometric electrochemical biosensor to detect UA in the concentration range of 0.1–200 μM, with a low limit of detection of 0.025 μM. Experimental results testify high selectivity, sensitivity and stability toward efficient detection of UA over potential interferents, revealing high detection accuracy and repeatability. The explored biosensor shows superior detection performances in real biological samples, together with high detection recoveries. Excellent properties and functions endow the biosensor with great prospects for precise screening and early diagnosis of UA-relevant malignant diseases in clinic.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Metal-organic framework
Electrodeposition
Boron nanosheets
Uric acid
Ratiometric electrochemical

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphologic and microstructural characterizations of products
Optimal experiment conditions for electrochemical measurements
Construction and performance of a ratiometric electrochemical biosensor
Detection performances of this biosensor in practical biological samples

Natural Science Foundation of Shandong, China
Natural Science Foundation of Shandong , China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong , China ( ZR2019MB026 ) and the Source Innovation Plan Application Basic Research Project of Qingdao , China ( 18-2-2-26-jch ).

This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong, China (ZR2019MB026) and the Source Innovation Plan Application Basic Research Project of Qingdao, China (18-2-2-26-jch).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of the as-prepared BNSs (a), BNSs-DOX (b), MOF (c), UOx@MOF (d) and UOx@MOF/BNSs-DOX (e). (f) The locally magnified TEM images of UOx@MOF/BNSs-DOX from (e). (g) SEM images of UOx@MOF/BNSs-DOX and (h) its locally magnified SEM images from (g).
(a) Powder XRD patterns, (b) FT-IR spectra, (c) UV-vis absorption spectra, and (d) Raman spectra of the prepared BNSs, BNSs-DOX, MOF, UOx@MOF, and UOx@MOF/BNSs-DOX.

The full-scan XPS spectra of the prepared BNSs (a), MOF (c), and UOx@MOF/BNSs-DOX (e). (b) The high-resolution XPS spectra of B1s from curve (a), and (d) the high-resolution XPS spectra of Co2p from curve (c).

(a) CV and (b) EIS curves of bare GCE and GCE modified with the as-prepared BNSs-DOX complex, UOx@MOF composite, and UOx@MOF/BNSs-DOX nanohybrid, respectively. Fe(CN)6 3−/4– was used as an electrochemical redox probe for signal output.

SWV curves of UOx@MOF/BNSs-DOX/GCE sensing platform immersed in PBS (1 mM) electrolyte solution with addition of UA (0.12 mM), which was continuously incubated for 20 min at different temperatures (a), incubated for different times at 37 °C (c) or incubated in PBS with adjustable pH values for 20 min at 37 °C (e). A series of relationships between redox peak-current intensities of UA and DOX versus incubation temperatures (b) and incubation times (d). (f) Relationships between the redox peak-current intensity ratios of UA and DOX (I UA/I DOX) versus pH values.

(a) SWV curves of UOx@MOF/BNSs-DOX/GCE sensing platform immersed into PBS electrolyte solution with the addition of UA at different coexisting concentrations, measured under the optimal experiment conditions. (b) A well-plotting linear relationship of I UA/I DOX versus [UA]. (c) Redox peak-current intensities located at the redox potential of UA and (d) I UA/I DOX values, calculated based on the measured SWV curves of this sensing platform in PBS electrolyte solution with respective additions of each interferent, UA, the mixture of all interferents, and “UA plus mixture”. Final coexisting concentrations of each interferent, UA and the mixture were fixed to 1.5 mM, 0.15 mM, and 19.5 mM, respectively.

Schematic illustration of the preparation of UOx@MOF/BNSs-DOX/GCE sensing platform and its application as an artificial nano-enzyme platform for enzymatic and ratiometric electrochemical biosensing of UA.

head.ce:title: Urate oxidase-loaded MOF electrodeposited on boron nanosheet-doxorubicin complex as multifunctional nano-enzyme platform for enzymatic and ratiometric electrochemical biosensing

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Intellectual Property Research Institute, Qingdao University, Shandong, 266071, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Intellectual Property Research Institute, Qingdao University, Shandong, 266071, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a novel multifunctional nano-enzyme platform was developed and used for enzymatic and ratiometric electrochemical biosensing of uric acid (UA). Boron nanosheets (BNSs) were prepared through ultrasound-assisted liquid-phase exfoliation, followed by the loading of doxorubicin (DOX) to form BNSs-DOX complex. The complex was drop-casted on glassy carbon electrode (GCE) surface to prepare BNSs-DOX/GCE. Cobalt-based metal-organic framework (MOF) with encapsulation of urate oxidase (UOx) was in-situ copolymerized and electrodeposited on the BNSs-DOX surface to construct UOx@MOF/BNSs-DOX nanohybrid-modified GCE. The modified electrode serves as an artificial nano-enzyme sensing platform and presents multifunctional functions, including DOX-loaded BNSs carrier, UOx-enzyme immobilization, enzymatic redox and ratiometric electrochemical sensing of UA. The platform was explored as a new ratiometric electrochemical biosensor to detect UA in the concentration range of 0.1–200 μM, with a low limit of detection of 0.025 μM. Experimental results testify high selectivity, sensitivity and stability toward efficient detection of UA over potential interferents, revealing high detection accuracy and repeatability. The explored biosensor shows superior detection performances in real biological samples, together with high detection recoveries. Excellent properties and functions endow the biosensor with great prospects for precise screening and early diagnosis of UA-relevant malignant diseases in clinic.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, theoretical calculations indicate that borophene namely ultrathin boron nanosheets (BNSs), as one emerging member of elemental two-dimensional (2D) materials, can present excellent physiochemical properties. As well predicted theoretically, BNSs possess metallicity, serve as the lightest 2D metal reported so far, and can be used for the electronically tunable monolayer metals. Superior elasticity, flexibility, strength, optical transparency, super-conductivity and other properties endow 2D BNSs with promising applications, especially in hydrogen storage, field emission, superconductivity, composite reinforced components, flexible nanocomponents and optoelectronic devices [1–10], which have attracted extensive research interests in the nanoscience community currently. Theoretical studies of borophene and BNSs began at the end of last century, and both theoretical and experimental studies have increased rapidly in last few years. After theoretical exploration, borophene and BNSs can be prepared from particular synthetic strategies, mainly involving molecular beam epitaxy, chemical vapor deposition, liquid phase exfoliation (LPE), etc. After experimental preparation, various unique properties and functionalized modifications of borophene and BNSs have been studied, and further explored towards significant applications, such as energy storage, gas sensors, electro-catalysis, biomedical therapy and optoelectronics [11–18]. Previous theoretical calculation studies predict potential applications of borophene and BNSs for the detection of ethanol, hydrogen cyanide, DNA sequence, CO, NO, alcohol and n-butylamine vapors [19–24]. However, so far there is almost no literature that refers to experimental studies on borophene or BNSs-based chemo/bio-sensing applications.

In this context, we experimentally prepared a multifunctional BNSs-based platform for ratiometric electrochemical sensing of uric acid (UA). Firstly, few-layered BNSs were prepared via ultrasound-assisted LPE, followed by loading antibiotic broad-spectrum anticancer drug doxorubicin (DOX) to form BNSs-DOX complex. The complex was drop-casted onto glassy carbon electrode (GCE) surface to construct a BNSs-DOX/GCE modified electrode. Then, cobalt-based metal-organic frameworks (MOF, ZIF-67) was electrodeposited onto BNSs-DOX/GCE surface through in-situ copolymerization of Co(NO3)2·6H2O and 2-methylimidazole in the coexistence of urate oxidase (UOx), which led to the generation of UOx-loaded MOF (Scheme 1 ). The formed UOx@MOF/BNSs-DOX/GCE sensing platform serves as an artificial nano-enzyme platform and exhibits multifunctional functions, including as DOX-loaded BNSs carrier, UOx enzyme-immobilization into MOF matrix, specific UOx-enzymatic redox of UA, the synergistically improved UA redox (UOx enzymatic, conductive BNSs), and ratiometric electrochemical UA sensing (intrinsic DOX signal as the reference, external UA signal as the response). Although previous studies have reported different analysis methods for UA detection [25–38], as summarized and compared in Table S1 (see Electronic Supplementary Material, ESM) in detail, most of them performed UA detection through measuring single-signal intensities. The single-signal sensing mode for target detection depends on accurate measurements of signal intensities, which inevitably undergo potential influence from fluctuation of environmental conditions, sample preparation, background signals, instrumental errors and target-irrelevant factors [39–43]. The feasibility and practicability of single-signal sensing systems are weakened during the detection process of targets in practical samples.

By contract, dual-signal ratiometric sensing mode can overcome the limitations of single-signal sensing mode, as it adopts the dual-signal intensity ratios (not signal intensities) in response to target concentrations as the signal output to perform accurate detection of targets. Dual-signal ratiometric sensing systems have intrinsic signal self-calibration function and superior detection performance in practical samples, showing improved detection stability, repeatability and reliability. Previous studies have mentioned the dual-signal ratiometric detection of UA, but focus on ratiometric fluorescence detection through utilizing lanthanide-doped upconversion nanoparticles (NPs), silicon NPs, heteroatom-doped carbon dots, noble-metal nanoclusters, semiconductor and MXene quantum dots as the sensing materials [44–50]. Considering the unique merits from electrochemical sensors (miniaturized instruments, microscale samples, high sensitivity) and ratiometric sensing systems (built-in signal correction, minimal errors, accurate detection), this work put forward a new way to efficiently detect UA based on a ratiometric electrochemical sensing mode. Herein, a novel UOx@MOF/BNSs-DOX/GCE sensing platform was explored. This BNSs/MOF nanohybrid-based artificial enzyme platform has multifunctional functions, and enables specific enzymatic redox of UA and ratiometric electrochemical biosensing of UA by means of a well-plotting relationship between redox peak-current intensity ratio (I UA/I DOX) and the corresponding UA concentration [UA].

This section includes the details of chemicals, instruments, experiment procedures and supplementary tables. These corresponding contents are available in the file of Electronic Supplementary Material (ESM).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphologic and microstructural characterizations of products
ce:section.ce:para: Morphologies and microstructures of the products were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images. BNSs were prepared by ultrasound-assisted LPE and TEM image (Fig. 1 a) exhibits characteristic boron nanosheet structures with a lateral size of about 50–100 nm [11–16]. As for BNSs-DOX complex, TEM image (Fig. 1b) has partial enrichment and adhesion of nanosheets due to intermolecular interactions between BNSs and DOX. The prepared ZIF-67 Co-based MOF shows the almost uniform morphologies (Fig. 1c), in agreement with previous reports [51]. After encapsulation of UOx into MOF to form UOx-loaded MOF, the morphologies of MOF are well preserved (Fig. 1d), which are similar to the case of sulfur NPs-encapsulated MOF [51]. Through in-situ copolymerization, UOx@MOF was electrodeposited around BNSs-DOX surface. TEM images (Fig. 1e and f) and SEM images (Fig. 1g and h) of the resulting UOx@MOF/BNSs-DOX nanohybrid illustrate the combined morphologic features originating from rhombic dodecahedron and flake-like nanostructures, revealing the existence of BNSs and the formation of ZIF-67 MOF around BNSs.

Microstructures of the products were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns, Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra, UV-vis absorption spectra and Raman spectra (Fig. 2 ). Powder XRD patterns indicate the crystalline structures of different products (Fig. 2a), which match with the standard simulated XRD patterns of ZIF-67 crystals and the cases of previous reports [16,51,52]. Almost consistent XRD patterns reveal that the loading of UOx and the binding of BNSs or BNSs-DOX complex have slight effects on the crystalline structures of ZIF-67 MOF. In FT-IR spectra (Fig. 2b), BNSs exhibit characteristic stretching vibrations, such as B–B at 3000–2900 cm−1 and B–O–B at around 1050 cm−1 [16]. B–O–B vibration is attributed to the edge oxidization during ultrasound-assisted LPE of bulk boron to prepare BNSs. There is a sharp characteristic bond of Co–N at 425 cm−1. A band at ca. 1450 cm−1 is assigned to blending vibration of alkane C–H [51,52]. After the encapsulation of UOx and the combination of BNSs or BNSs-DOX, FT-IR spectra of the resulting UOx@MOF/BNSs-DOX nanohybrid display the combined characteristic bonds from the corresponding components, which indicate that there are almost unobservable chemical interactions among different components. ZIF-67 MOF without and with UOx loading presents similar UV-vis absorption spectra (Fig. 2c). There is a characteristic absorption band located at ∼590 nm, deriving from tetrahedral-coordinated Co2+ in ZIF-67 Co-based MOF matrix [52]. UV-vis absorption spectra of the nanohybrid show a slightly red-shifted characteristic absorption band, which is ascribed to the introduction of different components [11,14–16,52].

Raman spectra of ZIF-67 MOF show characteristic Raman peaks (Fig. 2d). The low-energy Raman bonds located at < 300 cm−1 are due to metal-ligand vibrational modes, indicating coordination interactions of 2-methylimidazole ligands with Co centers. After the combination of MOF with UOx, BNSs and BNSs-DOX, these characteristic Raman peaks of MOF are well retained and the components in UOx@MOF/BNSs-DOX nanohybrid have negligible effects on molecular vibrations, rotations and structures of MOF [1,52]. In addition, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was adopted to characterize chemical compositions of products. In Fig. 3 a and b, the full-scan XPS spectra of BNSs show some typical peaks of B1s, C1s and O1s. A high-resolution particular peak of B1s at 187.5 eV well matches with the characteristic of BNSs reported in previous studies [1,11,14,15]. The particular peak of B1s can be decomposed into two characteristic B–B band and B–O–B band. The full-scan XPS spectra of UOx@MOF/BNSs-DOX confirm the existence of B1s, C1s, N1s, O1s and Co2p surface elements (Fig. 3e). The high-resolution XPS spectra of MOF (Fig. 3c) exhibit two obvious shakeup bands at 797.0 eV and 781.0 eV (Fig. 3d), which are attributed to the characteristic peaks of Co2p1/2 and Co2p3/2, respectively. These results reveal the coexistence of two valence states (Co2+, Co+) in the prepared ZIF-67 Co-based MOF [52–54]. XPS spectra verify the successful preparation of BNSs and MOF.

Before the construction of a ratiometric electrochemical biosensor of UA based on UOx@MOF/BNSs-DOX/GCE sensing platform, the electrode modification process of GCE surface with various substances can be characterized by measuring electrochemical signal responses, such as CV and EIS curves. Bare GCE or GCE with surface modification serving as the working electrode was immersed in PBS (1 mM, pH 7.0) electrolyte solution containing 0.1 M of KCl and 5 mM of Fe(CN)6 3−/4– as redox signal probe. CV curves of the bare GCE and modified GCE were measured and redox signal responses were analyzed (Fig. 4 a). BNSs-DOX/GCE has a higher cathodic peak current than that of bare GCE and UOx@MOF/GCE, revealing the excellent electroactive activity of BNSs and the improved electrochemical redox performance. After in-situ copolymerization and electrodeposition of MOF onto BNSs-DOX surface, the as-prepared UOx@MOF/BNSs-DOX/GCE has a relatively low cathodic peak current, because the binding interactions of different components partially block the charge transfer route during the redox process of Fe(CN)6 3−/4– ionic probes on GCE interface. Besides, the interface properties of bare GCE and modified GCE were characterized by measuring EIS curves (Fig. 4b). In general, the charge-transfer resistance (R ct) is closely relevant to the electron transfer kinetics of redox probes at electrode interface. The value of R ct can be calculated based on semicircle diameters of the Nyquist diagram. In contrast to bare GCE, the larger (or smaller) semicircle domains suggest the resistance (or acceleration) actions of charge transfer after surface modifications. By contrast, R ct of BNSs-DOX/GCE is lower than that of bare GCE and UOx@MOF/GCE, while R ct of UOx@MOF/BNSs-DOX/GCE is relatively higher than BNSs-DOX/GCE. The change trends are opposite obviously, because peak current and R ct are a couple of reciprocal parameters. Thus, CV and EIS curves testify the preparation process of UOx@MOF/BNSs-DOX/GCE sensing platform.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimal experiment conditions for electrochemical measurements
ce:section.ce:para: Before electrochemical measurements, optimization estimation of experimental conditions should be conducted to achieve sensitive signal curves of UOx@MOF/BNSs-DOX/GCE platform in response to the target. This platform as the working electrode was immersed into PBS electrolyte solutions with external addition of UA at a final coexisting concentration of 120 μM. SWV curves of electrolyte solutions were measured under different incubation conditions. With increase of incubation temperature, the redox peak-current intensities of UA (I UA) initially rise and then reach the maximum at 37 °C (Fig. 5 a), but a further increased temperature leads to sharp decrease of I UA. During the process of increasing incubation temperature, the redox peak-current intensities of DOX (I DOX) have slight changes (Fig. 5b). Upon the extending of incubation time, similar change trends of I UA are observed. I UA increases in the beginning and reach the maximal value at 20 min (Fig. 5c and d). A continuous extending of incubation time causes a dramatic reduction of I UA. Considering that DOX was loaded into UOx@MOF/BNSs-DOX nanohybrid on GCE surface, the fluctuation of external electrolyte conditions cannot produce obvious effects on DOX redox. Thus, I DOX is relatively insensitive to the fluctuation of incubation temperatures and times within specific ranges. Through using I DOX as a reference, the ratio values of I UA and I DOX (I UA /I DOX) were calculated based on the measured SWV curves at different pH conditions (Fig. 5e and f). At first, pH increase triggers the rising of I UA /I DOX and the maximum is achieved at pH 7.4. Afterwards, a further increased pH induces obvious decline of I UA /I DOX. In terms of experimental results above, optimal conditions for sensitive electrochemical measurements (high redox peak current and I UA /I DOX) involve the continuous incubation of PBS electrolyte solutions (pH 7.4) containing the target for 20 min at 37 °C.

To check the synergistically improved UA redox, MOF/GCE, UOx@MOF/GCE, BNSs/GCE, BNSs-DOX/GCE and UOx@MOF/BNSs-DOX/GCE were respectively immersed into the PBS (1 mM, pH 7.4) electrolyte solution, followed by the addition of UA (0.2 mM). After incubation for 20 min at 37 °C, the SWV curves (Fig. S1 in ESM) of various modified sensing platforms were measured under optimal experiment conditions. By contrast, UOx@MOF/BNSs-DOX/GCE sensing platform shows much high cathodic peak current, revealing a superior UA redox capability. In comparison with the cases of UOx@MOF/GCE and BNSs-DOX/GCE, UOx@MOF/BNSs-DOX/GCE exhibits a synergistically improved capability toward electrochemical redox of UA. To evaluate main diffusional/adsorptive processes on the electrode interface, the kinetic studies of UOx@MOF/BNSs-DOX/GCE modified electrode surface were performed through measuring the peak current changes under different scanning rates (Fig. S2a in ESM). With the increase of scanning rate from 25 mV s−1 to 200 mV s−1, the peak current regularly rises and moves towards a higher potential. Based on electrochemical principles, surface adsorptive/diffusional controlling process of electrode surface can be investigated by proportional relationships of the peak-current intensities with scanning rate (Fig. S2b in ESM) or its quadratic power (Fig. S2c in ESM). There are well-plotted proportional relationships between I p vs. R s (mV s−1), and I p vs. (R s)1/2 (mV s−1)1/2. The results reveal that UOx@MOF/BNSs-DOX/GCE modified electrode shows marked surface adsorptive controlling and diffusional controlling electrode processes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction and performance of a ratiometric electrochemical biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The prepared UOx@MOF/BNSs-DOX/GCE platform as a new working electrode was utilized for electrochemical signal curve measurements under the optimal experiment conditions. The platform was immersed in PBS (1 mM, pH 7.4) electrolyte solution with addition of UA. With each increase of coexisting [UA], the corresponding SWV curves were measured and I UA rises regularly because of the concentration-dependent redox of UA (Fig. 6 a). There is a well-plotted (R 2 = 0.9909) linear curve relationship between I UA /I DOX and [UA] in the specific range of 0.1–200 μM (Fig. 6b). Limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be approximately 0.025 μM (signal-to-noise S/N = 3) [27,38]. Based on the linear relationship of I UA /I DOX versus [UA], a novel ratiometric electrochemical biosensor of UA was developed based upon UOx@MOF/BNSs-DOX/GCE sensing platform for efficient detection of UA. The detection performance of this biosensor was evaluated through comparing with that of previously reported analysis methods, as summarized in Table S1 (see ESM). By comparison, the as-developed ratiometric electrochemical biosensor shows an appropriate linear detection range of [UA] and a lower LOD, surpassing most of previously reported analysis methods. Especially, this proposed biosensor of UA exhibits the combined advantages of sensitive electrochemical biosensing, dual-signal ratiometric biosensing and specific enzymatic biosensing [55]. The unique merits endow this biosensor with excellent detection accuracy, high repeatability, feasibility and practicability during practical detection applications in real biological samples.

Usually, the normal UA levels in fresh human serum and human urine fluids are 130–460 μM and 1.49–4.46 mM, respectively [56,57]. To adapt for the levels, real serum and urine fluids can be 100-fold diluted with PBS to prepare samples. The UA levels are properly adjusted to 1.3–4.6 μM and 14.9–44.6 μM, which are fully covered by the linear [UA] detection range of 0.1–200 μM. Thus, this biosensor can perform the detection of UA in despite of physiological samples from potential patients with the elevated or reduced UA levels [38]. Currently, the level of UA is widely used as a measureable indicator of diseases in clinical diagnosis, such as gout, hyperuricemia or Lesch-Nyhan syndrome, etc. Furthermore, detection performances of this biosensor were further assessed by conducting selectivity, sensitivity and stability assays. In detail, UOx@MOF/BNSs-DOX/GCE sensing platform was immersed in PBS (1 mM, pH 7.4) electrolyte solution with respective additions of UA, each interferent, mixture of all interferents, and “UA + mixture”. Then, SWV curves of the corresponding electrolyte solutions were measured under optimal conditions. Redox peak-current intensities located at the redox potential of UA (I UA) and I UA/I DOX were calculated and compared (Fig. 6c and d). In contrast to UA with marked changes of I UA (31.25) and I UA/I DOX (1.35), the addition of each interferent only leads to slight changes of I UA (<4.0) and I UA/I DOX (<0.17). Although the mixture of all interferents (19.5 mM) and UA (0.15 mM) were added together, the changes of I UA (34.70) and I UA/I DOX (1.43) are close to the case of only adding UA. The results demonstrate that each interferent (10-fold) and even all interferents (130-fold) at a higher coexisting concentration than UA cannot result in obvious influence on the detection of UA, revealing superior selectivity and sensitivity toward UA over potential interferents in biological samples. In addition, the sensing platform was covered with a beaker and placed in the dark, maintaining continuous incubation for 15 days. After that, a clean surface of the modified electrode was retained always. After the measurements of SWV curves with six repetitions to calculate and compare the redox peak-current intensities of UA, the relative standard deviation (RSD) was calculated to be 2.2%. Low RSD confirms superior detection repeatability and stability of this sensing platform.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection performances of this biosensor in practical biological samples
ce:section.ce:para: The constructed UOx@MOF/BNSs-DOX/GCE platform has a great potential as a new ratiometric electrochemical biosensor of UA. To assess its detection performances, this sensing platform was applied to the determination of UA from practical biological samples, including fresh human serum and urine fluid samples. Each biological fluid sample without or with external addition of UA was mixed with PBS electrolyte solution to prepare a series of sample mixture solutions. SWV curves of sample mixture solutions were measured. The values of [UA] were calculated based on the plotted linear relationship of I UA /I DOX versus [UA] (Fig. 6b), and rationally compared with the added (spiked) values of [UA]. As summarized in Table S2 (ESM), the detected values of [UA] are in well agreement with the added ones and HPLC methods, accompanied with detection recoveries of 96.98–104.30% and RSD values of 1.75–4.82%. High detection recoveries and low RSD verify that this ratiometric electrochemical biosensor of UA constructed based on UOx@MOF/BNSs-DOX/GCE sensing platform owns high feasibility and practicability for efficient determination of UA in practical biological samples.

In summary, a novel ratiometric electrochemical biosensor of UA was constructed based upon UOx@MOF/BNSs-DOX/GCE sensing platform. BNSs were prepared by ultrasound-assisted LPE, followed by loading DOX to generate BNSs-DOX complex. The complex was drop-casted on GCE surface to form BNSs-DOX/GCE modified electrode. Then, Co-based ZIF-67 MOF and UOx were in-situ copolymerized and electrodeposited onto the modified electrode to form the UOx@MOF/BNSs-DOX nanohybrid-modified GCE. The nanohybrid/GCE serves as an artificial nano-enzyme platform and has multifunctional functions, including DOX-loaded carrier, UOx immobilization, enzymatic redox and ratiometric electrochemical sensing. Morphologies and microstructures of the products were characterized systematically. Electrochemical curves were measured to characterize the surface modification process of electrodes. The optimal conditions for electrochemical measurements were investigated to obtain sensitive signal curves of this sensing platform in response to the target. A ratiometric electrochemical biosensor of UA was explored and enables linear detection of [UA] in 0.1–200 μM, with a low LOD of 0.025 μM. Experimental results confirm high selectivity, sensitivity and stability toward efficient detection of UA surpassing potential interferents, showing excellent detection accuracy and repeatability. In real human serum and urine fluid samples, the biosensor exhibits well agreement with the spiked results, high detection recoveries and low RSD, indicating excellent detection feasibility and practicability. Considering UA acts as a measureable indicator of diseases in clinical diagnosis, the superior detection performances endow the proposed biosensor with promising applications in precise screening and clinical diagnosis of malignant diseases and cancers at the early stage.

Here, the authors (Meng Yang, Zejun Sun, Hui Jin, Rijun Gui) declare that this manuscript is original research paper and is only submitted to the journal of Talanta . We do not submit this manuscript to other any journal for its publication. The research work in our manuscript is original and does not refer to any academic dishonourable behaviors. The authors declare no competing financial interest. We also declare that we have no financial and personal relationships with other people and organizations that can inappropriately influence our work. There is no professional and other personal interest of any nature product, service and company that can be construed as influencing the review of our present manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong, China (ZR2019MB026) and the Source Innovation Plan Application Basic Research Project of Qingdao, China (18-2-2-26-jch).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2022.123359.
